text: Recent examples have shown that iron based bimetal iron-metal-nitrogen-carbon (FeMNC) compounds present a high activity towards the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acidic conditions, whereas their structural properties and the possible synergy between the metallic centers remain unknown. We present here a comparative structural and catalytic study of FeSnNC and FeCoNC, in relation to their monometallic counterpart FeNC and SnNC by means of CO cryo-chemisorption, which evidenced a reduced density of M-N<sub>x</sub>><sub> </sub>sites by a factor close to two for FeSnNC and FeCoNC in relation to the parent FeNC material and FeNC presents a TOF about 50–100% higher than that of FeNC, thus implying that the presence of a second metallic center in these bimetallic compounds really does induce some improvement of the catalytic properties towards ORR. It was evidenced by electron microscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy that the metallic centers in both FeSnNC and FeCoNC compounds are present under the form of isolated metallic species: the iron species are evidenced by XAS as being associated with iron–nitrogen species similar to those of iron in FeNC, the tin and cobalt metallic centers, on the other hand, being detected exclusively in the EELS spectra. The Mössbauer analysis confirms that the presence of the second metal species in iron based bimetallic compounds affects the structural properties of iron in the active phase in relation to FeNC and shows that the presence of iron under the form of Fe-N<sub>4</sub> centers with a D<sub>1</sub> characteristic spectrum is significantly higher in FeSnNC and FeCoNC compounds, the increase in the D<sub>1</sub>/D<sub>2</sub> ratio when going from FeNC to the two iron bimetallic compounds, indicating that iron centers in a D<sub>1</sub> environment are promoted in these bimetallic systems which catalytic properties are enhanced in relation to those of the FeNC catalyst.
headline: Fully Human Written
